perturbation.original_text: Any notice required under this Agreement to be given by either Party to the other Party shall be in writing in the English language. Any required notice shall be effective two business days after it is sent by a recognized international courier service to the address of the other Party stated in this Agreement, or such other address as shall be notified to the other Party in writing, and any receipt issued by the courier service shall be conclusive evidence of the fact and date of sending of any such notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice required under this Agreement to be given by either Party to the other Party shall be in writing in the English language. Any required notice shall be effective a reasonable time after it is sent by a courier service to the address of the other Party stated in this Agreement, or such other address as may be convenient, and any confirmation of sending by the courier service may be considered as evidence of the fact and date of sending of any such notice.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'two business days' with 'a reasonable time,' which is ambiguous and subjective. It also changes 'recognized international courier service' to 'a courier service' which opens the door to using services with questionable tracking. The phrase 'may be convenient' makes the address to send the notice to unclear, which violates standards of legal notification.
perturbation.law_explanation: 735 ILCS 5/2-202 outlines the requirements for personal service, which necessitates clear and unambiguous delivery methods and addresses to ensure due process. Replacing a 'recognized international courier service' with the generic phrase 'a courier service' introduces ambiguity, as it does not specify the level of reliability or tracking required for the service. The ambiguous phrasing 'a reasonable time' and 'may be convenient' makes the addresses to send the notice to unclear, which conflicts with the requirements for proper notification, potentially invalidating the service of notice under Illinois law, because service of notice cannot be open to multiple interpretations. The added language creates an opportunity for manipulation or non-compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Illinois General Assembly - Illinois Compiled Statutes Home Legislation & Laws Senate House My Legislation Site Map Bills & Resolutions Compiled Statutes Public Acts Legislative Reports IL Constitution Legislative Guide Legislative Glossary Search By Number (example: HB0001) Search Tips Search By Keyword Search Tips Advanced Search Illinois Compiled Statutes ILCS Listing Public Acts Search Guide Disclaimer Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. 735 ILCS 5/2-202 (735 ILCS 5/2-202) (from Ch. 110, par. 2-202) Sec. 2-202. Persons authorized to serve process; place of service; failure to make return. (a) Process shall be served by a sheriff, or if the sheriff is disqualified, by a coroner of some county of the State. In matters where the county or State is an interested party, process may be served by a special investigator appointed by the State's Attorney of the county, as defined in Section 3-9005 of the Counties Code. A sheriff of a county with a population of less than 2,000,000 may employ civilian personnel to serve process. Process in any county may be served without special appointment by a person who is licensed or registered as a private detective under the Private Detective, Private Alarm, Private Security, Fingerprint Vendor, and Locksmith Act of 2004 or by a registered employee of a private detective agency certified under that Act. A private detective or licensed employee must supply the sheriff of any county in which he serves process with a copy of his license or certificate; however, the failure of a person to supply the copy shall not in any way impair the validity of process served by the person. The court may, in its discretion upon motion, order service to be made by a private person over 18 years of age and not a party to the action. It is not necessary that service be made by a sheriff or coroner of the county in which service is made. If served or sought to be served by a sheriff or coroner, he or she shall endorse his or her return thereon, and if by a private person the return shall be by affidavit. (a-3) In a county of 3,000,000 or more, any person who is licensed or registered as a private detective under the Private Detective, Private Alarm, Private Security, Fingerprint Vendor, and Locksmith Act of 2004 or by a registered employee of a private detective agency certified under that Act and hired to serve any summons originating in such county in the State shall pay $5 of each service fee, as establ
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities regarding notice requirements, potentially undermining legal certainty. The original text specifies 'two business days' for notice effectiveness and a 'recognized international courier service,' while the changed text uses 'a reasonable time' and 'a courier service.' This shift introduces subjectivity and reduces the reliability of the notification process. According to the Law Explanation, 735 ILCS 5/2-202 requires clear and unambiguous delivery methods to ensure due process. Replacing specific terms with vague alternatives like 'a reasonable time' and 'may be convenient' creates an opportunity for manipulation or non-compliance. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 speaks to who can serve process rather than specifics of delivery, the Law Explanation makes it clear that the ambiguity in the changed text conflicts with requirements for proper notification.

perturbation.original_text: All matters relating to arbitration will be governed by the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. Sections 1 et seq.). Except to the extent governed by the Federal Arbitration Act or other federal law, this Agreement and all claims arising from the relationship between Hyatt (and/or any of its Affiliates) and Playa (and/or any of its Affiliates) under this Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of Illinois (U.S.A.), without regard to its conflict of laws rules, except that any Illinois law or any other law regulating the offer or sale of franchises, business opportunities, or similar interests, or governing the relationship between a franchisor and a franchisee or any similar relationship, will not apply unless its jurisdictional requirements are met independently without reference to this Section 4.
perturbation.changed_text: All matters relating to arbitration will be governed by the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. Sections 1 et seq.). Except to the extent governed by the Federal Arbitration Act or other federal law, this Agreement and all claims arising from the relationship between Hyatt (and/or any of its Affiliates) and Playa (and/or any of its Affiliates) under this Agreement will be generally guided by the laws of the State of Illinois (U.S.A.), taking into account its conflict of laws rules, except that any Illinois law or any other law regulating the offer or sale of franchises, business opportunities, or similar interests, or governing the relationship between a franchisor and a franchisee or any similar relationship, will not apply unless its jurisdictional requirements are met independently without reference to this Section 4.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'will be governed' to 'will be generally guided' and adds 'taking into account its conflict of laws rules'. This makes the governing law less certain and introduces ambiguity, potentially contradicting the principle of contractual certainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois choice of law rules and the principle of contractual certainty
perturbation.law_citation: Illinois choice of law rules and the principle of contractual certainty
perturbation.law_explanation: Illinois law, following general principles of contract law, respects the parties' choice of law provision in a contract. However, the changes introduce ambiguity by stating the agreement will be 'generally guided' by Illinois law, rather than 'governed'. This weakens the enforceability and predictability of the chosen law. By stating that Illinois laws are only 'generally guiding', it creates uncertainty about whether they're actually binding, potentially creating a conflict in what is considered a legally binding jurisdiction. This goes against Illinois and general US Law, where contractual agreements must abide by the clearly chosen law.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation changes the mandatory 'will be governed' to the more ambiguous 'will be generally guided,' introducing uncertainty about the binding nature of Illinois law. According to the Law Explanation, this weakens enforceability and predictability, potentially creating a conflict regarding the legally binding jurisdiction. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction,' where an ambiguous obligation makes it difficult to enforce a legal obligation under state or national law, potentially leading to non-compliance.
